subject: elevenplus
title: What are your favourite and least favourite subj
question: What are your favourite and least favourite subjects, and why?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: What would make your least favourite more enjoyable?

Ask for the reasons behind both, and a specific topic they enjoyed or struggled with. If they're only positive, gently explore the harder subject to see how they handle challenge.

What to look for: Curiosity and a willingness to tackle difficulty matter more than only naming strengths.

subject: elevenplus
title: Tell me about a topic you have studied recently
question: Tell me about a topic you have studied recently that really interested you.
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: What made it fascinating?

Let their curiosity lead — ask what surprised them or what they'd like to learn next about it. Follow their enthusiasm; this is a strong chance for them to shine, so give plenty of space.

What to look for: Curiosity and a willingness to tackle difficulty matter more than only naming strengths.

subject: elevenplus
title: When you find a piece of work difficult
question: When you find a piece of work difficult, how do you approach it?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: Tell me about a time this worked.

Steer towards a real example rather than a general strategy. Notice resilience and problem-solving, and ask how they felt when they finally cracked it.

What to look for: Curiosity and a willingness to tackle difficulty matter more than only naming strengths.

subject: elevenplus
title: Which subject do you think will be most useful l
question: Which subject do you think will be most useful later in life, and why?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: Can you argue for a different one too?

Encourage them to reason it through and consider more than one answer. If they give a confident single choice, invite the opposite case to see flexible thinking.

What to look for: Curiosity and a willingness to tackle difficulty matter more than only naming strengths.

subject: elevenplus
title: If you could invent a brand-new subject to be ta
question: If you could invent a brand-new subject to be taught at school, what would it be?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: What would pupils learn in it?

Play along with their invention and ask for detail — what a lesson would look like, why it matters. Follow their creativity; there are no wrong answers here.

What to look for: Curiosity and a willingness to tackle difficulty matter more than only naming strengths.

subject: elevenplus
title: What is your greatest achievement so far
question: What is your greatest achievement so far, and why does it mean so much to you?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: What did you have to do to get there?

Reassure them that it can be big or small. Follow up on the effort and feelings behind it — the struggle, the moment of success, why it stuck with them. Let them relive it.

What to look for: Big or small doesn't matter — what matters is that they can explain why it means so much.

subject: elevenplus
title: What is the biggest challenge you have ever face
question: What is the biggest challenge you have ever faced, and how did you deal with it?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: What would you do differently next time?

Handle sensitively; let the child choose how much to share. Follow their lead on how they coped and what they learned. Focus on resilience and reflection, not the difficulty itself.

What to look for: Big or small doesn't matter — what matters is that they can explain why it means so much.

subject: elevenplus
title: Tell me about a time you did not succeed at firs
question: Tell me about a time you did not succeed at first. What did you learn?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: How did you keep going?

Encourage honesty about setbacks and draw out the learning and persistence. Reassure them that not succeeding first time is normal, so they feel safe to be candid.

What to look for: Big or small doesn't matter — what matters is that they can explain why it means so much.

subject: elevenplus
title: What would you like to do when you are an adult
question: What would you like to do when you are an adult, and what draws you to it?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: What might you need to learn to get there?

Follow the 'why' rather than the job title, and reassure them it's fine not to know yet. If they're unsure, ask what kinds of things they enjoy that might point somewhere.

What to look for: Answers shouldn't sound rehearsed — encourage genuine reasons behind the ambition.

subject: elevenplus
title: Is there a problem in the world you would like t
question: Is there a problem in the world you would like to help solve one day?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: Why does that one matter to you?

Explore what sparked their concern and any personal connection. Follow their reasoning with genuine interest — this reveals values and awareness, so give them room to think aloud.

What to look for: Answers shouldn't sound rehearsed — encourage genuine reasons behind the ambition.

subject: elevenplus
title: What clubs or activities do you take part in out
question: What clubs or activities do you take part in outside lessons?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: Which is your favourite, and why?

Pick one activity they mention and dig in — how they got into it, what they love, any highlights. If the list is short, that's fine; depth on one is better than a long list.

What to look for: Enthusiasm and commitment over time say a lot — draw out what they've stuck with.

Explore the motivation behind the interest and how they've explored it so far. Keep the focus on the child's curiosity rather than any particular school.

What to look for: Enthusiasm and commitment over time say a lot — draw out what they've stuck with.

subject: elevenplus
title: Tell me about an activity you have stuck with fo
question: Tell me about an activity you have stuck with for a long time. What has kept you going?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: How have you improved?

Draw out the story of their progress — early struggles, a proud moment, what keeps them motivated. Commitment is the theme, so let them reflect on their journey.

What to look for: Enthusiasm and commitment over time say a lot — draw out what they've stuck with.

subject: elevenplus
title: Have you ever tried an activity that was complet
question: Have you ever tried an activity that was completely new to you? How did it go?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: Would you try something new again?

Follow up on how they felt facing something unfamiliar and what they learned. If it went badly, gently explore what they took from it — openness to new things is what matters.

What to look for: Enthusiasm and commitment over time say a lot — draw out what they've stuck with.

subject: elevenplus
title: If you could start a brand-new club at school
question: If you could start a brand-new club at school, what would it be and why?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.

Follow-up you can use: Who would you hope joins?

Follow their idea with genuine interest — what they'd do, how they'd run it, why it excites them. This reveals initiative and interests, so give room to imagine.

What to look for: Enthusiasm and commitment over time say a lot — draw out what they've stuck with.

subject: elevenplus
title: Tell me about your family. Do you have any broth
question: Tell me about your family. Do you have any brothers or sisters?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: How do you get on with them?

If the child names siblings or family members, follow up on the relationships they mention — who they are closest to, what they do together, any funny or fond stories. If they give a short list, warmly invite them to describe one person in more detail so they can open up.

What to look for: Encourage full answers using Point, Explanation, Evidence — not one-word replies.

subject: elevenplus
title: How do you like to spend time together as a fami
question: How do you like to spend time together as a family?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: Is there something you always do at weekends or holidays?

Latch onto any specific activity or ritual they raise and ask what they enjoy about it or how it makes them feel. If the answer is vague, offer a gentle prompt (meals, trips, games, weekends) to help them find something concrete to talk about.

What to look for: Encourage full answers using Point, Explanation, Evidence — not one-word replies.

subject: elevenplus
title: What is a family tradition that is special to yo
question: What is a family tradition that is special to you, and why?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: How does it make you feel?

Explore the 'why' behind whatever tradition they choose — who started it, what happens, why it matters. If they can't think of a formal tradition, reassure them and ask about something they simply always do together, however small.

What to look for: Encourage full answers using Point, Explanation, Evidence — not one-word replies.

subject: elevenplus
title: Who in your family do you go to when you need he

answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.

Follow-up you can use: Can you give an example?

Ask for a real example of a time this person helped them, to move from a name to a story. Notice what kind of help they value (emotional, practical, academic) and follow that thread.

What to look for: Encourage full answers using Point, Explanation, Evidence — not one-word replies.

subject: elevenplus
title: If your family could go anywhere in the world to

Follow the reasoning behind their choice rather than the destination itself — what draws them there, what they imagine doing, who they picture with them. This reveals interests and imagination, so give them space to expand.

What to look for: Encourage full answers using Point, Explanation, Evidence — not one-word replies.

subject: elevenplus
title: What do you like to do in your free time
question: What do you like to do in your free time?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: What is it about that you enjoy most?

Pick up on whatever they mention and ask them to describe a recent time they did it. If they name several things, invite them to choose the one they're most passionate about and expand.

What to look for: Aim for a few things they can discuss with real enthusiasm and specific detail.

subject: elevenplus
title: You're trapped on a desert island and can take o
question: You're trapped on a desert island and can take one luxury item. What do you take, and why?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:

Keep this light and playful. Follow the reasoning behind their choice — it often reveals a hobby or interest. Gently push for the 'why' if they only name the item.

What to look for: Aim for a few things they can discuss with real enthusiasm and specific detail.

subject: elevenplus
title: If you had a whole day with no plans and no rule
question: If you had a whole day with no plans and no rules, how would you spend it?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: Why would that be a perfect day?

Let their answer paint a picture and follow the details — who's there, where they are, what they'd do first. This is a window into their personality, so keep it relaxed and curious.

What to look for: Aim for a few things they can discuss with real enthusiasm and specific detail.

subject: elevenplus
title: Tell me about what you are reading at the moment
question: Tell me about what you are reading at the moment.
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: What do you think will happen next, and why?

Follow the specific book they name: ask what's happening, what they think of a character, or how they'd feel in that situation. If they aren't reading anything now, ask about the last book they enjoyed instead.

What to look for: Depth beats breadth — knowing one book well is more impressive than listing many.

subject: elevenplus
title: What is your favourite book
question: What is your favourite book, and what makes it special to you?

modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: Which character do you relate to most?

Go deeper on the one book rather than moving on — favourite moment, why it stayed with them, how it made them feel. Let them lead you into the parts they're most excited to share.

What to look for: Depth beats breadth — knowing one book well is more impressive than listing many.

subject: elevenplus
title: What do you think makes a good story
question: What do you think makes a good story?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: Can you give an example of a book that does this well?

Ask them to test their own idea against a book they know. If they mention 'exciting' or 'interesting' characters, ask what makes a character interesting to help them articulate their thinking.

What to look for: Depth beats breadth — knowing one book well is more impressive than listing many.

subject: elevenplus
title: Why do you think reading is important

answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: Has a book ever changed how you see something?

Invite a personal example — a book that taught them something or changed their mind. Follow whatever reason they give (imagination, learning, escape) and ask them to expand with a real instance.

What to look for: Depth beats breadth — knowing one book well is more impressive than listing many.

subject: elevenplus
title: If you could meet any character from a book
question: If you could meet any character from a book, who would it be, and what would you ask them?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: What do you admire about them?

Explore what the choice says about them — what they'd ask, and why that character appeals. Keep it playful and follow their imagination wherever it goes.

What to look for: Depth beats breadth — knowing one book well is more impressive than listing many.

subject: elevenplus
title: What is your favourite thing about your current
question: What is your favourite thing about your current school?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: Why does that matter to you?

Whatever they name — a subject, friends, a club, a place — ask them to describe a specific moment or example. If they answer in one word, gently ask 'what is it about that you like?' to help them articulate.

What to look for: Look for warmth about their current school and a sense of what they give back to it.

subject: elevenplus
title: If you were headteacher for a day
question: If you were headteacher for a day, what is one thing you would change, and why?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: How would that help other pupils?

Probe the reasoning: who benefits, and are there any downsides? Invite them to think it through out loud rather than expecting a polished answer — the aim is to see how they reason and consider others.

What to look for: Look for warmth about their current school and a sense of what they give back to it.

subject: elevenplus
title: Do you think school rules are necessary? Which r
question: Do you think school rules are necessary? Which rule do you think matters most?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: Can you argue both sides?

Encourage balanced thinking — 'on the one hand… on the other'. If they pick a side quickly, invite them to consider the opposite view. Follow up on any personal example that prompted their opinion.

What to look for: Look for warmth about their current school and a sense of what they give back to it.

subject: elevenplus
title: Tell me about a teacher who has made a differenc
question: Tell me about a teacher who has made a difference to you.
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: What did they do that stuck with you?

Draw out a concrete example of something the teacher did or said. Notice what the child values in an adult (kindness, high standards, humour) and reflect that back to keep them talking.

What to look for: Look for warmth about their current school and a sense of what they give back to it.

subject: elevenplus
title: What do you contribute to your school community
question: What do you contribute to your school community?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: Do you have any responsibilities or roles?

If they hold a role or help others, ask for a specific instance. If they're modest or unsure, reassure them and prompt gently about small everyday things they do for classmates or the school.

What to look for: Look for warmth about their current school and a sense of what they give back to it.

subject: elevenplus
title: If I were interviewing your best friend
question: If I were interviewing your best friend, how would they describe you?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: Can you give an example that shows that?

Ask for a real situation that demonstrates each quality they name, turning labels into stories. If they're shy about praising themselves, gently reassure them it's okay to talk about their good points.

What to look for: Look for self-awareness — backed up with a real example rather than just labels.

subject: elevenplus
title: What is one thing other people don't know about
question: What is one thing other people don't know about you?
answer: Get-to-know-you question — there is no single correct answer. Assess how well the child articulates and expresses themselves, with specific detail and honesty.
modelReasoningPath:
Follow-up you can use: Why haven't many people seen this side of you?

Keep it warm and low-pressure. Follow whatever they reveal with curiosity and no judgement, giving them space to share a hidden interest, talent, or side of themselves.

What to look for: Look for self-awareness — backed up with a real example rather than just labels.
